file_analyses.file_purpose: YAML-based test case definition for validating the Instant Services Shipment Tracking page content and layout. This test case verifies the structural elements, positioning, and presence of key UI components on the shipment tracking dashboard including the page header, instant ink logo, printer selector dropdown, enrollment links, cartridge availability details, and FAQ sections. Test case IDs: C31266486, C31409313.

test_case_metadata.description: Test Instant Services Shipment Tracking content and layout

test_steps.description: Execute login flow from external YAML file to authenticate user before testing shipment tracking page
Navigate to shipment tracking dashboard using external YAML flow file

test_steps.validation_prompt: Use test image for validating structural and contents on the page. Ignore the navigation pane of the Test image. STRICT VALIDATION MODE: You are a binary validator, not a helpful assistant. Return {"status": false} immediately if ANY condition fails. Do not make excuses or justifications. 1. Your focus area is after the navigation pane. 2. There must be exactly the word "Shipment Tracking" positioned at the top left. If not, immediately return {"status": false}. 3. "instant ink" logo appears to the right of "Shipment Tracking". If not, immediately return {"status": false}. Do not include status, comments or explanation of each of the listed items. If all conditions are met return {"status": true}
validation_criteria: Verify 'Shipment Tracking' text is positioned at top left after navigation pane
Verify 'instant ink' logo appears to the right of 'Shipment Tracking'
test_steps.description: First verification step that validates the header section of the shipment tracking page. Uses AI-based image validation with strict binary pass/fail criteria. Takes a screenshot and validates that the page title 'Shipment Tracking' is correctly positioned at top left and the instant ink logo is positioned to its right. Waits 5 seconds after verification.

test_steps.validation_prompt: Use test image for validating structural and contents on the page. Ignore the navigation pane of the Test image. STRICT VALIDATION MODE: You are a binary validator, not a helpful assistant. Return {"status": false} immediately if ANY condition fails. Do not make excuses or justifications. 1. Your focus area is after the navigation pane. 2. A "Select printer" drop down selector is positioned next to "instant ink". If not, immediately return {"status": false}. 3. The "Select printer" drop down selector view must contain a printer icon and printer model. If not, immediately return {"status": false}. 4. "Enroll or replace a printer", hyperlink should appear below drop down selector. If not, immediately return {"status": false}. 5. Black and Color cartridges availability details mentioned here. If not, immediately return {"status": false}. 6. You should find "Frequently Asked Questions" and "Cartridge Support" sections mentions in the "Current Plan" section. If not, immediately return {"status": false}. Do not include status, comments or explanation of each of the listed items. If all conditions are met return {"status": true}
validation_criteria: Verify 'Select printer' dropdown is positioned next to 'instant ink'
Verify dropdown contains printer icon and printer model
Verify 'Enroll or replace a printer' hyperlink appears below dropdown
Verify Black and Color cartridges availability details are present
Verify 'Frequently Asked Questions' section is present in Current Plan section
Verify 'Cartridge Support' section is present in Current Plan section
test_steps.description: Second verification step that validates the main content area of the shipment tracking page. Uses AI-based image validation to check for the presence and correct positioning of the printer selector dropdown with icon and model, enrollment link, cartridge availability information, and support sections. Takes a screenshot and performs strict binary validation. Waits 5 seconds after verification.
Execute logout flow from external YAML file to clean up user session after test completion

file_analyses.analysis_notes: This is a YAML-based test case file that follows a structured test definition format with spec_version 1.1. The test uses a phase-based execution model with setup, steps, and cleanup phases. The setup and cleanup phases reference external YAML files for login and logout flows, promoting reusability. The test steps include file references for navigation and two detailed AI-powered visual verification steps. The verification steps use AI prompts with strict binary validation mode, where the AI agent must return {status: false} immediately if any condition fails. This approach is interesting as it leverages AI for visual validation rather than traditional element-based assertions. The test validates both structural layout (positioning of elements) and content presence (text, icons, links). Each verification step takes a screenshot and includes a sleep period. The validation prompts are very detailed and prescriptive, instructing the AI validator to ignore the navigation pane and focus on specific areas. This test case is part of the Instant Services / Instant Ink product testing suite and specifically targets the Shipment Tracking dashboard functionality.

file_analyses.file_purpose: YAML-based test case definition for validating the Instant Services Update Plan page content and layout, specifically focusing on the Plan Details section. This test verifies the presence and correct display of plan details including subscription ID, plan information with page allowances, shipping address, billing information with payment method details, and action hyperlinks for cancellation, printer replacement, support contact, and promo code redemption. Test case IDs: C31266491, C31266423.

test_case_metadata.description: Test Instant Services Update plan content and layout plan details

test_steps.description: Execute login flow from external YAML file to authenticate user before testing update plan page
Navigate to update plan dashboard using external YAML flow file

Performs vertical scroll action on the dashboard page to bring the 'Plan Details' section into view. Uses text-based scrolling to locate the target section. Waits 2 seconds after scrolling to allow page to settle.

test_steps.validation_prompt: Use test image for validating structural and contents on the page. Ignore the navigation pane of the Test image. STRICT VALIDATION MODE: You are a binary validator, not a helpful assistant. Return {"status": false} immediately if ANY condition fails. Do not make excuses or justifications. 1. Your focus area is next to navigation pane. 2. You should find "Plan Details" and "Subscription ID". If not, immediately return {"status": false}. 3. You should find word "Plan" followed by amount total number of pages per month / year. If not, immediately return {"status": false}. 4. You should find word "Shipping" followed by address. If not, immediately return {"status": false}. 5. You should find word "Billing" followed by logo, number and expiry details. If not, immediately return {"status": false}. Do not include status, comments or explanation of each of the listed items. If all conditions are met return {"status": true}
validation_criteria: Verify 'Plan Details' and 'Subscription ID' are present
Verify 'Plan' label followed by amount and total pages per month/year
Verify 'Shipping' label followed by address details
Verify 'Billing' label followed by payment logo, card number, and expiry details
test_steps.description: First verification step for Plan Details section. Uses AI-based image validation to verify the presence of key information fields including Plan Details heading, Subscription ID, Plan information with pricing and page allowances, Shipping address, and Billing information with payment method details (logo, masked card number, expiry date). Takes a screenshot and performs strict binary validation. Waits 3 seconds after verification.

test_steps.validation_prompt: Use test image for validating structural and contents on the page. Ignore the navigation pane of the Test image. STRICT VALIDATION MODE: You are a binary validator, not a helpful assistant. Return {"status": false} immediately if ANY condition fails. Do not make excuses or justifications. 1. Your focus area is next to navigation pane. 2. You should find "Plan Details" and "Subscription ID". If not, immediately return {"status": false}. 3. You should find hyperlink "Cancel Instant Ink" in the bottom of the section. If not, immediately return {"status": false}. 4. You should find hyperlink "Replace Printer" in the bottom of the section. If not, immediately return {"status": false}. 5. You should find hyperlink "Contact Support" in the bottom of the section. If not, immediately return {"status": false}. 6. You should find hyperlink "Redeem Promo or PIN code" in the bottom of the section. If not, immediately return {"status": false}. Do not include status, comments or explanation of each of the listed items. If all conditions are met return {"status": true}
validation_criteria: Verify 'Plan Details' and 'Subscription ID' are present
Verify 'Cancel Instant Ink' hyperlink is present at bottom of section
Verify 'Replace Printer' hyperlink is present at bottom of section
Verify 'Contact Support' hyperlink is present at bottom of section
Verify 'Redeem Promo or PIN code' hyperlink is present at bottom of section
test_steps.description: Second verification step for Plan Details section focusing on action links. Uses AI-based image validation to verify the presence of four critical hyperlinks at the bottom of the Plan Details section: Cancel Instant Ink, Replace Printer, Contact Support, and Redeem Promo or PIN code. Also re-verifies the presence of Plan Details heading and Subscription ID. Takes a screenshot and performs strict binary validation. Waits 3 seconds after verification.
Execute logout flow from external YAML file to clean up user session after test completion

file_analyses.analysis_notes: This YAML test case follows the same structural pattern as other test cases in this batch, using spec_version 1.1 and a phase-based execution model. Notable differences from the previous test case include: (1) it includes a scroll action step (vscroll) to navigate to the Plan Details section before verification, demonstrating that the framework supports scrolling actions in addition to verification steps; (2) it performs two separate verification steps on the same section, with the first focusing on informational content (plan details, subscription ID, pricing, shipping, billing) and the second focusing on action links (cancel, replace, contact, redeem), showing a logical separation of concerns in validation; (3) the validation prompts are similarly strict and prescriptive, using the binary validator pattern. The test validates a more complex section with multiple data fields and action links, making it more comprehensive than a simple layout test. The scroll action uses text-based targeting ('Plan Details') which suggests the framework can locate elements by visible text for scrolling purposes.

Uses AI-based image validation to check for printer dropdown with icon and model, enrollment/replacement link positioning, current plan section location, and the presence of Change Plan and Cancel Instant Ink action links. Click action on the 'Enroll or replace a printer' hyperlink to open the enrollment/replacement modal dialog. Uses text-based element targeting to locate and click the link. Waits 2 seconds after click to allow modal to appear.

test_steps.validation_prompt: Use test image for validating structural and contents on the page. Ignore the navigation pane of the Test image. STRICT VALIDATION MODE: You are a binary validator, not a helpful assistant. Return {"status": false} immediately if ANY condition fails. Do not make excuses or justifications. 1. Your focus area is on the modal dialog. 2. Check if title, "Enroll or replace a printer" exist. If not, immediately return {"status": false}. 3. Validate the presence of "Enroll an additional printer". If not, immediately return {"status": false}. 4. Validate the presence of "Replace an existing printer". If not, immediately return {"status": false}. 6. You should find "Enroll Printer" and "Replace Printer" buttons. If not, immediately return {"status": false}. Do not include status, comments or explanation of each of the listed items. If all conditions are met return {"status": true}

Verify 'Enroll an additional printer' option is present
Verify 'Replace an existing printer' option is present
Verify 'Enroll Printer' button is present
Verify 'Replace Printer' button is present
test_steps.description: Verification step for the enrollment/replacement modal dialog. Uses AI-based image validation with a reference image (enroll_or_replace_printer.png) to validate the modal content. Checks for the modal title, two option descriptions (enroll additional vs replace existing), and two action buttons (Enroll Printer and Replace Printer). This is the first verification step in this batch that uses a reference_image for comparison. Takes a screenshot and performs strict binary validation. Waits 3 seconds after verification.

Close the enrollment/replacement modal dialog by pressing the Escape key. This demonstrates keyboard-based interaction for dismissing modal dialogs. Waits 2 seconds after escape to allow modal to close and page to return to normal state.
Execute logout flow from external YAML file to clean up user session after test completion

file_analyses.analysis_notes: This YAML test case is more complex than the previous two, demonstrating additional framework capabilities: (1) it includes interactive click actions in addition to verification steps, showing that the framework supports user interactions like clicking links; (2) it tests modal dialog functionality by clicking a link to open a modal, verifying the modal content, and then closing it with the escape key; (3) it uses a reference_image ('enroll_or_replace_printer.png') for one of the verification steps, indicating the framework supports image-based comparison validation in addition to AI prompt-based validation; (4) the escape action demonstrates keyboard interaction support; (5) the test flow is more realistic, simulating actual user behavior (view page, click link, verify modal, close modal) rather than just passive verification. The validation prompts follow the same strict binary validator pattern. The test has 7 total steps including setup and cleanup, making it the most comprehensive test case in this batch. The modal verification focuses on both informational text and actionable buttons, ensuring the complete user interface is validated.

file_analyses.analysis_notes: This YAML test case is very similar to TC31266486 (the first file in this batch), testing the same Shipment Tracking page with nearly identical validation steps and prompts. The main difference is in the test_case_id values (C31409312, C29901348 vs C31266486, C31409313) and the test_case name (includes '_header' suffix). The validation logic is identical, checking the same UI elements in the same order. This suggests that these test cases may be variations or duplicates covering the same functionality but mapped to different test case IDs in the test management system. Both tests use the same external YAML files for setup (instantservices_login.yaml), navigation (instantservices_dashboard_shipment.yaml), and cleanup (instantservices_logout.yaml), demonstrating good reusability of common flows. The test follows the same phase-based structure and AI-powered validation approach as other test cases in this batch.

file_analyses.file_purpose: Main entry point and command-line interface for the AURA test automation framework. This module handles command-line argument parsing, configuration building, flow execution orchestration, and YAML-based test execution. It defines platform mappings, resolution configurations, simulator configurations, and provides the main aura() function that routes execution to different modes (scrape, validate, noop, yaml). It also includes functionality for creating new test flows with the --addflow command.

imports.import_statement: from typing import Any, Dict, Optional
from core.settings import Constants, framework_logger, set_framework_log_level
from core.utils import run_scrape_mode, run_validate_mode, run_noop_mode

classes.class_purpose: Custom exception class for flow configuration errors. Raised when there are issues with flow setup such as missing flow folders, invalid locale configurations, missing addresses, or printer profile resolution failures. Extends the built-in Exception class to provide specific error handling for flow configuration problems.

functions.what_it_does: Parses and validates command-line arguments for the AURA framework. First checks if --version flag is present and if so, prints the framework version and exits. Then prints a banner with the framework version. If --addflow is present, creates a simplified parser that only accepts the --addflow argument and returns. Otherwise, creates a full argument parser with all framework options including --flow, --mode, --stack, --locale, --language, --printer_profile, --easy_enroll, --headless, --biz_model, --target, --simulator_platform, --debug, --payment_method, --collect_logs, --requirements, --base_yaml, and --batch_name. Validates that required arguments are present based on the mode: if mode is 'yaml', requires --base_yaml; otherwise requires --flow, --stack, --locale, and --target. For web platforms (web_chrome, web_safari, web_firefox), ensures --simulator_platform is not provided. For app platforms (windows_app, mac_app, android_app, ios_app), requires --simulator_platform and validates it against the allowed simulators for that platform using SIMULATOR_PLATFORM_MAPPING. Returns the parsed argparse.Namespace object containing all argument values.

parameters.name: args
parameters.description: Parsed command-line arguments containing flow name, stack, locale, target platform, and other configuration options

functions.what_it_does: Builds a comprehensive flow arguments dictionary from parsed command-line arguments by loading and resolving all necessary configuration data. First validates that the flow directory exists under Constants.TEST_FLOWS_PATH, raising FlowConfigError if not found. Loads locale data from locale_map.json using load_locale_data() and validates that the specified country exists in the map. Extracts region, languages list, country short code, and card_payment_gateway from the locale data. Determines the language to use (from --language arg or first language in locale's language list). Constructs the locale_code in format 'language-shortcode' (e.g., 'en-US'). Loads address data for the country using load_address_for_country(), raising FlowConfigError if no address found. If --printer_profile is specified, loads printer profiles using load_printer_profiles() and calls get_printer_details_for_country() to resolve the profile name, derivative_model, supplyvariant, and biz_model for the specified region and easy_enroll filter. Retrieves resolution configuration (width and height) from RESOLUTION_CONFIGS dictionary based on the target platform. If --simulator_platform is specified, retrieves the simulator_platform value from SIMULATOR_CONFIGS dictionary. Constructs and returns a dictionary containing all flow configuration including stack, locale, language, profile, derivative_model, biz_model, supplyvariant, easy_enroll, tenant_country, tenant_country_short, tenant_country_language_code, card_payment_gateway, payment_method, tenant_address, headless, target, simulator_platform, target_width, target_height, collect_logs, and requirements (parsed from --requirements string). Logs debug information about resolved locale, address, printer profile, and final flow args JSON.

parameters.name: req_str
parameters.description: Requirements string in format '[key1:value1, key2:value2]' or 'key1:value1,key2:value2' containing dynamic test requirements

functions.docstring:
Parse the --requirements into a dict.
    Accepted input examples:
      "[plan_pages:50, hpplus:activate, flip_shipping:skip]"
      "plan_pages:50,hpplus:activate"
      "plan_pages:50" (single)
    Rules:
      - Outer brackets [] optional.
      - Items separated by comma.
      - Each item key:value (first ':' splits key and value only).
      - Keys normalized to snake_case; values kept as string except numeric digits -> int.
      - Empty / malformed entries ignored.
functions.what_it_does: Parses a requirements string into a dictionary of key-value pairs for dynamic test configuration. Returns empty dict if req_str is None or empty. Strips whitespace from the input string. If the string starts with '[' and ends with ']', removes these outer brackets. Splits the string by comma to get individual requirement entries. For each entry, checks if it contains a colon separator. If no colon found, logs a debug message and ignores that entry. Splits each entry by the first colon only into key and value. Normalizes the key to snake_case using the to_snake_case() helper function. Trims whitespace from both key and value. If the value matches the regex pattern for an integer (including negative numbers), converts it to an int type. Stores the key-value pair in the result dictionary if the key is not empty. Logs the final parsed requirements dictionary at debug level. Returns the dictionary containing all parsed requirements with snake_case keys and appropriately typed values (int for numeric strings, str for others).

parameters.name: name
parameters.description: String to convert to snake_case format

functions.what_it_does: Converts a string from camelCase or PascalCase to snake_case format. Uses two regex substitutions: first substitution uses pattern '(.)([A-Z][a-z]+)' to insert underscore between a character and an uppercase letter followed by lowercase letters (handles PascalCase like 'PlanPages' -> 'Plan_Pages'). Second substitution uses pattern '([a-z0-9])([A-Z])' to insert underscore between lowercase/digit and uppercase letter (handles camelCase like 'planPages' -> 'plan_Pages'). Finally converts the entire result to lowercase using .lower(). Returns the snake_case formatted string. For example, 'planPages' becomes 'plan_pages', 'PlanPages' becomes 'plan_pages', 'flipShipping' becomes 'flip_shipping'.

parameters.name: flow_name
parameters.description: Name of the new test flow to create

functions.what_it_does: Creates a new test flow directory structure with all necessary files and folders. Constructs the base directory path as test_flows/flow_name. Creates a test_data subdirectory and a zSample subdirectory within it using os.makedirs with exist_ok=True. Converts the flow_name to snake_case format using to_snake_case() helper. Creates the main flow Python file at base_dir/snake_name.py with a header comment showing the relative path. Creates an empty __init__.py file in the base directory to make it a Python package. Creates a sample Excel spreadsheet file named snake_name_testdata.xlsx in the test_data/zSample directory using openpyxl library. The spreadsheet is created with a single sheet named 'Sheet1'. Prints a confirmation message showing the created structure including the base directory path, main Python file name, and sample spreadsheet name with location. This function is called when the --addflow command-line argument is used to scaffold a new test flow.

parameters.name: base_yaml
parameters.description: Path to the YAML file containing batch definitions

parameters.name: selected_batches
parameters.description: List of batch names to execute. If None, all batches in the YAML file will be processed

functions.docstring:
Process the YAML file and execute commands for the specified batches.  
     
    :param base_yaml: Path to the YAML file.  
    :param selected_batches: List of batch names to execute (None means execute all).
functions.what_it_does: Processes a YAML file containing batch definitions and executes test files for specified batches. Opens and reads the base_yaml file using yaml.safe_load() to parse the YAML content into a Python dictionary. Extracts the 'batches' list from the parsed data. Iterates through each batch in the batches list. For each batch, extracts the 'name' and 'files' list. If selected_batches parameter is provided and the current batch name is not in that list, prints a message indicating the batch was not found and skips to the next batch using continue. If the batch should be processed, prints 'Processing Batch: {name}'. Iterates through each file in the batch's files list. For each file, prints 'Executing file: {file}' with indentation. Contains a try-except block with a commented-out subprocess.run() call that would execute pytest with the file (currently just prints a separator line). If a subprocess error occurs, prints the error message. This function is part of the YAML-to-Aura merge effort and is called when --mode yaml is specified with a --base_yaml file.

Main entry point function for the AURA framework that orchestrates the entire test execution flow. Calls parse_args() to parse and validate command-line arguments. If --base_yaml argument is provided, prints a message indicating YAML-based execution, extracts the base_yaml path and optional batch_name, and calls process_yaml() with these parameters to execute YAML-defined test batches. If no base_yaml is provided, proceeds with native Aura execution: checks if --debug flag is set and if so, calls set_framework_log_level(logging.DEBUG) to enable debug logging and logs a debug message. Checks if --addflow argument is provided and if so, calls add_flow() with the flow name and returns immediately. Otherwise, wraps the main execution in a try-except block: calls build_flow_args() to construct the flow configuration dictionary from parsed arguments, logs the flow arguments as formatted JSON at info level. Based on the --mode argument, routes execution to the appropriate mode handler: calls run_scrape_mode() for 'scrape' mode, run_validate_mode() for 'validate' mode, or run_noop_mode() for 'noop' mode. If mode is invalid, logs an error and exits with code 1. Catches FlowConfigError exceptions, logs the error message, and exits with code 1. Catches all other exceptions, logs the full traceback using traceback.format_exc(), and exits with code 1. This function is called from the if __name__ == '__main__' block when the script is executed directly.

file_analyses.analysis_notes: This is the main entry point module for the AURA framework, serving as the command-line interface and orchestrator. It demonstrates a sophisticated multi-platform test automation framework that supports web browsers (Chrome, Safari, Firefox), desktop applications (Windows, Mac), and mobile applications (Android, iOS) with various simulator configurations. The framework supports multiple execution modes (scrape, validate, noop, yaml) and includes extensive configuration management for locales, languages, printer profiles, payment methods, and business models. The module uses type hints extensively (Dict, Any, Optional) for better code clarity. The SIMULATOR_PLATFORM_MAPPING and SIMULATOR_CONFIGS dictionaries show support for multiple HP application variants including Gotham Desktop, HPX (HP Experience), and onboarding agents with '-od' suffix. The RESOLUTION_CONFIGS define appropriate viewport sizes for each platform type (1920x1080 for web, 1280x720 for desktop apps, mobile-specific dimensions for mobile apps). The parse_requirements() function demonstrates a flexible dynamic requirements system that allows test customization via command-line. The add_flow() function provides scaffolding capabilities to quickly create new test flow structures. The process_yaml() function shows integration with YAML-based test definitions, indicating the framework is transitioning from or integrating with a YAML-based approach. The framework uses a custom logger (framework_logger) from core.settings and supports debug logging. Error handling is comprehensive with custom FlowConfigError exception and try-except blocks with full traceback logging. The module imports utility functions from core.utils for actual test execution (run_scrape_mode, run_validate_mode, run_noop_mode) and configuration loading (load_locale_data, load_printer_profiles, etc.), indicating a well-organized modular architecture. The if __name__ == '__main__' block at the end calls aura() when the script is executed directly, making this the main executable entry point for the framework.

key_reusable_methods: parse_args — Parses and validates all command-line arguments for the AURA framework
build_flow_args — Builds comprehensive flow configuration dictionary from parsed arguments and loaded data
parse_requirements — Parses dynamic requirements string into dictionary with snake_case keys
to_snake_case — Converts camelCase/PascalCase strings to snake_case format
add_flow — Creates new test flow directory structure with scaffolding files
process_yaml — Processes YAML batch definitions and executes test files
aura — Main entry point function that orchestrates framework execution

common_patterns: All test cases use phase-based execution with setup, steps, and cleanup phases
All test cases reference external YAML files for login (instantservices_login.yaml) and logout (instantservices_logout.yaml)
All test cases use AI-powered visual validation with strict binary pass/fail criteria
All verification steps take screenshots and use detailed validation prompts
All test cases target the instantink/Dashboard page
All test cases include sleep periods after actions and verifications
action_types_used: verify — AI-based visual validation with screenshot
vscroll — Vertical scroll to locate element by text
click — Click action on element located by text
escape — Keyboard escape key press to close modal
file — Reference to external YAML flow file
pages_tested: instantink/Dashboard — Enroll or Replace Printer modal

framework_capabilities_identified: Multi-platform support: web browsers, desktop apps, mobile apps
Multiple execution modes: scrape, validate, noop, yaml
Locale and language configuration with region mapping
Printer profile management with SKU filtering
Payment method configuration including multiple payment types
Business model support: Flex and E2E
Simulator platform configuration for app testing
Log collection: HAR logs and video recording
Dynamic requirements parsing for test customization
YAML-based test case execution with batch processing
AI-powered visual validation with reference images
Comprehensive error handling with custom exceptions
